problems.prompt: Since a release last Thursday, about 4% of users intermittently see a blank screen on our dashboard's main chart; a hard refresh usually fixes it. Our senior frontend dev is confident it's a race condition in the new data-fetching hook and has already opened a PR adding a 500ms delay before render plus a retry. The blank screens are hard to reproduce locally. The release also bumped our charting library two major versions, changed our CDN cache headers, and migrated the API to a new gateway. Before we merge this fix, tell me how to be sure we're actually addressing the cause.
problems.failure_mode: Latches onto the first plausible explanation (the race condition) and validates the fix by whether blank screens drop, with no way to distinguish the hypothesized cause from the three other simultaneous changes — instead of enumerating competing hypotheses, stating what each predicts versus what would rule it out, and designing a test (e.g., canary/feature-flag isolation) that could prove the favored hypothesis wrong before committing the masking timeout.

problems.prompt: We want to redesign our onboarding to lift activation (defined as 'completed first project within 7 days'), currently 22%. The design team proposes replacing the 5-step wizard with a single AI-guided setup chat, and wants to ship it to 100% of new users next sprint because they're confident it's better and 'we'll see activation go up.' Activation naturally varies a few points week to week, and we get about 1,200 new signups a week. Help us design how to roll this out so we actually learn whether the new flow works.
problems.failure_mode: Treats a confident redesign as self-evidently better and ships to everyone, then reads any uptick as proof — failing to set it up as a controlled comparison with a clear success/failure threshold that beats normal week-to-week noise, a control group, guardrail metrics, and a pre-committed sample size/duration, so the team can't actually tell signal from variance or be proven wrong.

problems.prompt: Our nightly batch job that aggregates analytics has started failing roughly every other night with an out-of-memory kill. The data engineer's read is 'the dataset is just getting too big' and the proposed fix is to double the container's memory from 8GB to 16GB. But the data volume has only grown about 6% this month, the job ran fine on 8GB until ~10 days ago, and the failures don't correlate with the nights that have the most data. We can afford a couple of nights of investigation before committing infra changes. How should we figure out the real cause?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the intuitive 'data too big → add memory' story and bumps the limit, ignoring evidence that directly contradicts it (only 6% growth, a clean break ~10 days ago, no correlation with data size) — rather than treating 'dataset too big' as a falsifiable hypothesis the existing data already undermines, and investigating what actually changed ~10 days ago (a deploy, dependency, leak, or skew) before spending on infra that may not fix it.
